steps.title: Understand the task: compare two women's contributions
steps.content: The notes describe two distinct roles at the 1963 March on Washington: Hedgeman worked behind the scenes to ensure a woman was included in the speaker lineup, while Bates was the woman who actually spoke. The correct choice must capture both sides of this comparison simultaneously — not just mention one woman, and not blur their distinct contributions.

steps.title: Eliminate each choice
steps.content:
• Choice A — Hedgeman and Bates contributed to the march in different ways; Bates, for example, delivered a brief but memorable address.: Mentions both women but never states what Hedgeman actually did — no comparison is possible without both sides. ✗
  • Choice B — Hedgeman worked in politics and helped organize the march, while Bates was a journalist and school desegregation advocate.: Gives background on each woman but says nothing about Bates's contribution to the march itself. ✗
  • Choice C — Although Hedgeman worked behind the scenes to make sure a woman speaker was included, Bates was the sole woman to speak at the march.: Directly contrasts Hedgeman's behind-the-scenes organizing with Bates's on-stage speech — both contributions, one comparison. ✓
  • Choice D — Many African American women, including Bates and Hedgeman, fought for civil rights, but only one spoke at the march.: Lumps the two women together as generic civil rights fighters and never distinguishes their individual march contributions. ✗
That is the correct answer.
